question: Q1
title: errorhandler middleware active unconditionally — full stack traces exposed to clients
exact_code:
const errorhandler = require('errorhandler')
...
app.use(verify.errorHandlingChallenge())
app.use(errorhandler())
...
errorhandler.title = `${config.get<string>('application.name')} (Express ${utils.version('express')})`
data_flow: Any unhandled Express error → verify.errorHandlingChallenge() (challenge bookkeeping only) → errorhandler() → sends full HTML error page including stack trace, file paths, and Express/Node version to the HTTP client. The errorhandler package is explicitly designed for development use and its own README warns it must never be used in production.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker sends a malformed JSON body or triggers any route that calls next(err). 2. errorhandler responds with an HTML page containing the full Node.js stack trace, absolute file paths on disk, internal module names, and the Express version string. 3. The test in httpSpec.ts line 45 confirms this: a GET to /rest/x returns status 500 with '<title>Error: Unexpected path: /rest/x</title>' and a full stack trace body. 4. This information directly aids path-traversal, dependency-version fingerprinting, and targeted exploit selection.
framework_protection: errorhandler should only be loaded when process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development'. In production Express apps the correct pattern is a four-argument (err, req, res, next) middleware that logs internally and returns a generic 500 response with no internal detail.
fix_recommendation: Wrap the errorhandler() call: if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development') { app.use(errorhandler()); } else { app.use((err, req, res, next) => { logger.error(err); res.status(500).json({ status: 'error', message: 'An unexpected error occurred.' }); }); }

question: Q2
title: Admin account seeded with hardcoded password 'admin123' on every startup
exact_code:
- email: admin
  password: 'admin123'
  key: admin
  role: 'admin'
data_flow: server.ts calls start() → datacreator() → createUsers() → loads users.yml → UserModel.create({ email: 'admin@<domain>', password: 'admin123', role: 'admin' }). The admin account is recreated from scratch on every sequelize.sync({ force: true }) (line 724 server.ts), meaning the password is reset to 'admin123' on every server restart. The login route (routes/login.ts line 60) explicitly verifies this credential to mark the weakPasswordChallenge as solved, confirming the credential is functional.
exploit_scenario: 1. Application domain defaults to juice-sh.op (config). 2. Attacker posts to /rest/user/login with { email: 'admin@juice-sh.op', password: 'admin123' }. 3. Server returns a signed JWT with role:'admin'. 4. Attacker has full administrative access: can view all user data, orders, feedback, and access /administration panel.
framework_protection: Default/seed credentials for privileged accounts must be randomised at first-start and stored in a secrets manager, or forced-change on first login. The application must never ship with a known static admin password.
fix_recommendation: Replace the static password in users.yml with a crypto.randomBytes(32) value generated at seed time and written to a secure secret store (env var / vault). Add a 'mustChangePassword' flag and enforce rotation on first login for all admin accounts.

question: Q2
title: RSA private key hardcoded in source code (lib/insecurity.ts)
exact_code: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\nMIICXAIBAAKBgQDNwqLEe9wgTXCbC7+RPdDbBbeqjdbs4kOPOIGzqLpXvJXlxxW8iMz0EaM4BKUqYsIa+ndv3NAn2RxCd5ubVdJJcX43zO6Ko0TFEZx/65gY3BE0O6syCEmUP4qbSd6exou/F+WTISzbQ5FBVPVmhnYhG/kpwt/cIxK5iUn5hm+4tQIDAQABAoGBAI+8xiPoOrA+KMnG/T4jJsG6TsHQcDHvJi7o1IKC/hnIXha0...(truncated)\r\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
data_flow: The hardcoded privateKey is used by: (1) jwt.sign(user, privateKey, { expiresIn: '6h', algorithm: 'RS256' }) — minting all authentication JWTs; (2) crypto.createHmac('sha256', privateKey) in deluxeToken() — generating deluxe membership tokens. Anyone with access to the repository (public GitHub) can extract this key and forge arbitrary JWTs for any user including admin.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads the public repository and extracts the RSA private key from lib/insecurity.ts. 2. Attacker crafts a JWT payload: { data: { id: 1, email: 'admin@juice-sh.op', role: 'admin' } } signed with the known private key using RS256. 3. Attacker sends this token in the Authorization header. 4. expressJwt verifies it against the known public key (also file-readable) and grants admin access without needing a password.
framework_protection: Private keys must never be committed to source control. They should be loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime. The encryptionkeys/jwt.pub public key is correctly read from disk; the private key should follow the same pattern.
fix_recommendation: Remove the hardcoded key. Load it via: const privateKey = process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY ?? fs.readFileSync('encryptionkeys/jwt.key', 'utf8'). Rotate the key immediately since the current key is already compromised by its presence in version control.

question: Q2
title: Cookie parser uses hardcoded secret 'kekse'
data_flow: The cookieParser secret is used to sign and verify signed cookies. The string 'kekse' (German for 'cookies') is trivially guessable and publicly visible in source. Any signed cookie value can be forged by an attacker who knows this secret.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker reads the source or the repository. 2. Attacker uses the known secret 'kekse' to sign a cookie with a crafted payload. 3. If any route trusts req.signedCookies without further JWT validation, the attacker's forged value is accepted as authentic.
framework_protection: Cookie secrets must be randomised, stored in environment variables, and rotated periodically. They should never appear in source code.
fix_recommendation: Replace with: app.use(cookieParser(process.env.COOKIE_SECRET ?? crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'))). Set COOKIE_SECRET in the deployment environment.

question: Q3
title: CORS wildcard allows all origins — no origin restriction
data_flow: The cors() middleware with no configuration sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on every response. The test in httpSpec.ts line 15 explicitly asserts this: 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *'. This means any cross-origin script can make credentialed or non-credentialed requests to the API and read the responses.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim is logged into Juice Shop (has a valid JWT cookie or Authorization header). 2. Victim visits attacker's webpage. 3. Attacker's page uses fetch() to call https://juice-shop/rest/user/whoami and reads the JSON response including the user's email and account details because CORS returns *. 4. With a stolen JWT the attacker can place orders, export data, etc.
framework_protection: CORS origin should be restricted to the application's own domain. Use: cors({ origin: process.env.ALLOWED_ORIGIN ?? 'https://your-domain.com', credentials: true }). Wildcards are incompatible with credentials: true and expose all API data to arbitrary origins.
fix_recommendation: Replace app.use(cors()) with app.use(cors({ origin: config.get('server.baseUrl'), credentials: true })). Remove the comment that normalizes this as acceptable.

question: Q3
title: Content-Security-Policy absent at application level; only set on user profile page with 'unsafe-eval'
data_flow: helmet is imported in server.ts but only helmet.noSniff() and helmet.frameguard() are called. helmet.contentSecurityPolicy() is never invoked globally. The only CSP header set is on the /profile route, and it includes 'unsafe-eval' in script-src (allowing eval/Function() calls) and interpolates the user-controlled profileImage URL directly into the policy header, which can be abused to inject additional CSP directives. The xssFilter middleware is commented out at line 187.
exploit_scenario: 1. Most routes (all /api/* and /rest/* endpoints) return responses with no CSP header. 2. If an XSS payload is stored (e.g., via product review), the browser executes it freely because no CSP blocks inline scripts on those pages. 3. On the profile page, an attacker sets profileImage to a URL containing a semicolon followed by additional policy directives (e.g., script-src *) to weaken or bypass the partial CSP.
framework_protection: A strict Content-Security-Policy should be applied globally via helmet.contentSecurityPolicy() with specific directives. 'unsafe-eval' and 'unsafe-inline' should be avoided. The profile image URL must be validated and sanitized before interpolation into a header value.
fix_recommendation: Add globally: app.use(helmet.contentSecurityPolicy({ directives: { defaultSrc: ["'self'"], scriptSrc: ["'self'"], imgSrc: ["'self'", 'data:'], styleSrc: ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"] } })). Remove unsafe-eval from the profile CSP and validate profileImage against an allowlist before interpolating.

question: Q3
title: HSTS (HTTP Strict-Transport-Security) header not set
data_flow: helmet is imported but only noSniff and frameguard are activated. helmet.hsts() is never called. Without HSTS, browsers will not enforce HTTPS-only connections on subsequent visits. The application can be accessed over plain HTTP, enabling network-level interception of authentication tokens and session data.
exploit_scenario: 1. Application deployed behind a TLS terminator but without HSTS. 2. Attacker performs a network MITM (e.g., public WiFi). 3. Victim's browser follows an HTTP link to the shop (redirect from port 80). 4. Attacker intercepts the HTTP leg before the redirect, capturing the session token or injecting malicious content. 5. HSTS would prevent this by instructing the browser to always use HTTPS after the first visit.
framework_protection: helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 31536000, includeSubDomains: true, preload: true }) should be applied for all production deployments running behind TLS.
fix_recommendation: Add: app.use(helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 31536000, includeSubDomains: true })) inside the middleware block in server.ts. Ensure the application forces HTTPS redirect at the load-balancer level.

question: Q4
title: Directory listing enabled for /ftp, /encryptionkeys, /.well-known, and /support/logs
exact_code:
app.use('/ftp', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('ftp', { icons: true }))
app.use('/ftp(?!/quarantine)/:file', servePublicFiles())
app.use('/.well-known', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('.well-known', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/encryptionkeys', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/encryptionkeys/:file', serveKeyFiles())
app.use('/support/logs', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('logs', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))
app.use('/support/logs/:file', serveLogFiles())
data_flow: serve-index renders a browsable HTML directory listing. /ftp exposes arbitrary downloadable files. /encryptionkeys exposes JWT keys and other cryptographic material. /support/logs exposes rotating access logs (containing IP addresses, endpoints, cookies, and user-agent strings from all users). All four paths have no authentication middleware applied before serveIndex.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /encryptionkeys — attacker sees and downloads jwt.pub and potentially other key files. Combined with the hardcoded private key (MISCONFIG-003) this enables full JWT forgery. 2. Unauthenticated GET to /support/logs — attacker downloads timestamped access logs revealing all IP addresses, session tokens in query strings, and endpoint usage patterns. 3. Unauthenticated GET to /ftp — attacker browses and downloads backup files, configuration exports, or other sensitive documents stored there.
framework_protection: Directory listings must be disabled for production. Sensitive directories should require authentication middleware. Cryptographic key files must not be served over HTTP under any circumstances.
fix_recommendation: Remove serveIndex middleware from /encryptionkeys and /support/logs entirely. For /ftp, require authentication before listing. For /encryptionkeys, serve only the public key and only to authenticated admin users: app.use('/encryptionkeys', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin()). Delete serveIndex from that route.

question: Q5
title: Prometheus /metrics endpoint publicly accessible without authentication
data_flow: metrics.serveMetrics() (routes/metrics.ts line 884) calls register.metrics() from prom-client and sends the full Prometheus scrape payload. This includes: application version (exact semver), number of registered users, wallet balances, order counts, challenge solve status, cheat scores, and HTTP request rates by status code. No isAuthorized() middleware is applied. The comment 'vuln-line exposedMetricsChallenge' confirms this is intentional for the challenge but it is a real misconfiguration in any production context.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /metrics returns hundreds of lines of Prometheus metrics. 2. Attacker reads juice-shop_version_info to identify the exact application version for CVE matching. 3. Attacker reads user registration counts and wallet balances to understand the scale and value of accounts. 4. HTTP request rate metrics reveal endpoint usage patterns for targeted attack planning.
framework_protection: Metrics endpoints must be protected by network-level controls (only accessible from internal scrape IPs) or require authentication. In Express: app.get('/metrics', ipWhitelist(['10.0.0.0/8']), metrics.serveMetrics()) or require a Bearer token.
fix_recommendation: Add authentication or IP filtering: app.get('/metrics', security.isAuthorized(), metrics.serveMetrics()). Alternatively restrict the /metrics route to localhost or a private subnet via a reverse-proxy rule.

question: Q5
title: Full application configuration exposed via unauthenticated REST endpoint
data_flow: The route GET /rest/admin/application-configuration (server.ts line 605) calls retrieveAppConfiguration() which returns the entire node-config 'config' object as JSON. This object contains: application name and domain, server base URL, challenge parameters, feature flags, product definitions, and potentially any environment-overlaid secrets. There is no isAuthorized() or isAdmin() middleware applied to this route. Despite the '/admin/' path prefix, the route is fully public.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /rest/admin/application-configuration returns a large JSON blob. 2. Attacker reads challenge configurations, internal domain names, base URLs, and any keys stored in config files. 3. The configuration reveals which challenges are enabled, their expected key values, and internal routing information, enabling targeted attack planning.
framework_protection: Admin endpoints must require authentication and admin-level authorization. The /admin/ path prefix provides no security by itself — it requires explicit middleware.
fix_recommendation: Add authorization: app.get('/rest/admin/application-configuration', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin(), retrieveAppConfiguration()). Consider returning only non-sensitive configuration subsets rather than the full config object.

question: Q5
title: Swagger API documentation publicly accessible at /api-docs
data_flow: The Swagger UI is mounted at /api-docs with no authentication middleware. It renders the full swagger.yml which documents all B2B v2 API endpoints, their expected parameters, authentication requirements, and response schemas. This provides attackers with a complete attack surface map.
exploit_scenario: 1. Unauthenticated GET to /api-docs loads the full interactive API documentation. 2. Attacker identifies all available endpoints, HTTP methods, and parameter structures. 3. Attacker uses the 'Try it out' Swagger UI feature to make live API calls directly from the documentation page. 4. This significantly reduces the reconnaissance effort required for targeted attacks.
framework_protection: API documentation endpoints should be disabled in production or protected behind authentication. Access should be restricted to internal/developer networks.
fix_recommendation: In production builds: if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') { app.use('/api-docs', swaggerUi.serve, swaggerUi.setup(swaggerDocument)); } Or add: app.use('/api-docs', security.isAuthorized(), security.isAdmin(), swaggerUi.serve, swaggerUi.setup(swaggerDocument)).

question: Q6
title: Raw error objects serialized and returned to clients in multiple routes
data_flow: Multiple routes serialize the raw error object directly into the JSON response: likeProductReviews.ts line 56, updateProductReviews.ts line 27, createProductReviews.ts line 32. A MongoDB or Node.js Error object serialized via JSON.stringify() exposes: the error message (which may contain query details, collection names, or driver internals), the error code, and on some error types, a partial stack property. This leaks database schema and driver version information.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker sends a malformed NoSQL query to PUT /rest/products/:id/reviews (updateProductReviews). 2. The MongoDB driver throws an error with a message like 'Cast to ObjectId failed for value "x" at path "_id"'. 3. Server responds with res.status(500).json(err) — the full error object including message and code fields is returned. 4. Attacker learns the database is MongoDB, the field type is ObjectId, and can tailor a NoSQL injection payload accordingly.
framework_protection: Error handlers must log the full error internally and return only a sanitized generic message to the client. Use utils.getErrorMessage() consistently (which the codebase does in some routes) or a centralized error handler.
fix_recommendation: Replace res.status(500).json(err) with res.status(500).json({ error: 'An internal error occurred. Please try again.' }) in all three affected routes, and log the raw error with logger.error(err) for internal diagnostics.
